# Smithers

> Deterministic, resumable AI workflow orchestration using TOON and JSX.
> Source: https://smithers.sh
> GitHub: https://github.com/evmts/smithers
> Package: smithers-orchestrator on npm

This file contains the complete Smithers documentation. Each section below corresponds to a documentation page on smithers.sh.

---

## Introduction

> Choose the Smithers API that matches how you want to author workflows.
> Source: https://smithers.sh/introduction

Smithers supports two authoring APIs. They both compile to the same durable execution engine, but they optimize for different ways of building workflows.

## Choose an API

| API | Best for | Start here |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **TOON** | Prompt-first workflows and the fastest path to a working flow | [Overview](/toon/overview), [Installation](/toon/installation), [Quickstart](/toon/quickstart) |
| **JSX** | TSX composition, render-time branching, and MDX prompts | [Overview](/jsx/overview), [Installation](/jsx/installation), [Quickstart](/jsx/quickstart) |

## Shared Runtime

No matter which API you choose, Smithers handles the same runtime concerns:

- durable execution and resumability
- retries, timeouts, and approval gates
- SQLite persistence
- parallel execution and loops
- structured events, metrics, and observability

The sidebar sections for Core Concepts, Runtime, CLI, and Integrations are shared across both API tabs because they describe the common engine underneath the authoring layers.

## Next Steps

- [TOON Overview](/toon/overview) — Start with the declarative API.
- [JSX API](/jsx/overview) — Build workflows as TSX components.

---

## Installation

> Choose the installation guide for the Smithers API you want to use.
> Source: https://smithers.sh/installation

Smithers has separate installation guides for each authoring API.

## Pick an Installation Guide

| API | Use this when | Installation guide |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **TOON** | You want declarative `.toon` files and the least setup | [TOON Installation](/toon/installation) |
| **JSX** | You want TSX workflows, MDX prompts, and render-time branching | [JSX Installation](/jsx/installation) |

## Shared Requirements

Both APIs run on the same underlying runtime, so these show up everywhere:

- Bun
- local SQLite persistence
- optional model or provider credentials
- the shared runtime docs for observability, CLI usage, and integrations

## Next Steps

- [TOON Installation](/toon/installation) — Start with the declarative path.
- [JSX Installation](/jsx/installation) — Set up TSX and optional MDX prompts.

---

## Quickstart

> Choose the quickstart for the Smithers API you want to use.
> Source: https://smithers.sh/quickstart

Smithers has a different quickstart for each authoring API.

## Pick a Quickstart

| API | What you build | Quickstart |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **TOON** | A declarative `.toon` workflow with inline schemas and prompts | [TOON Quickstart](/toon/quickstart) |
| **JSX** | A TSX workflow with `<Task>` components and `ctx.outputMaybe(...)` | [JSX Quickstart](/jsx/quickstart) |

## Same Runtime, Different Authoring Style

Both quickstarts end up on the same runtime:

- steps are persisted to SQLite
- completed work resumes after restarts
- retries, approvals, and loops use the same engine
- observability and CLI tooling are shared

## Next Steps

- [TOON Quickstart](/toon/quickstart) — Start with the declarative flow.
- [JSX Quickstart](/jsx/quickstart) — Build a render-driven TSX workflow.

---

## Execution Model

> How Smithers turns a builder graph into a durable Effect workflow.
> Source: https://smithers.sh/concepts/execution-model

Smithers executes workflows as durable state machines built from a typed graph. The important split is:

- **workflow definition is pure**
- **step execution is effectful**
- **state transitions are durable**

That gives you deterministic orchestration without a render cycle.

## The Core Loop

Every workflow run follows the same high-level cycle:

```txt
1. Build      workflow definition -> typed graph
2. Materialize load execution state from SQLite
3. Select     find ready steps whose dependencies are satisfied
4. Execute    run each ready step as an Effect
5. Commit     persist outputs, attempts, events, and transitions
              -> repeat until terminal
```

Unlike the old JSX API, the workflow definition is not re-rendered after each step. The graph is already explicit.

## Phase 1: Build the Graph

The workflow graph can be produced from either of Smithers' two definition layers:

- **TOON** — a `.toon` file is compiled into a workflow graph at build time
- **JSX** — the React renderer extracts `TaskDescriptor` objects from a component tree

Both produce the same internal graph structure. The rest of the execution model is identical regardless of which layer defined the workflow.

A TOON workflow describes the graph declaratively:

```toon
name: review-loop
input:
  topic: string
steps[2]:
  - id: analyze
    run: "return { summary: `Analysis of ${input.topic}` };\n"
    output:
      summary: string
  - id: review
    run: "return { approved: true, feedback: 'LGTM' };\n"
    output:
      approved: boolean
      feedback: string
```

This phase is pure:

- it creates step handles
- it records dependency edges
- it records control-flow structure
- it records policies like retry, timeout, approval, and concurrency

No Effects run during graph construction.

## Phase 2: Materialize Execution State

When you call `Workflow.execute(input)`, Smithers:

1. validates the input against the declared `Schema`
2. allocates or resumes a durable execution id
3. loads persisted workflow state from SQLite
4. reconstructs the current step state machine

At this point Smithers knows:

- which steps have completed
- which outputs already exist
- which attempts failed
- which approvals are pending
- which loop or branch nodes are suspended

The graph is static. The execution state is what changes over time.

## Phase 3: Select Ready Steps

Smithers walks the graph and marks each step according to durable state.

Typical states are:

| State | Meaning |
| --- | --- |
| `pending` | Known to the workflow but not yet eligible to run |
| `ready` | All dependencies and guards are satisfied |
| `running` | The step Effect is currently executing |
| `waiting-approval` | Suspended on a durable approval gate |
| `completed` | Output has been persisted successfully |
| `failed` | Terminal failure after retry policy is exhausted |
| `cancelled` | Interrupted by the user or runtime |
| `skipped` | Not taken because of a branch or guard |

A step becomes `ready` only when:

- every entry in `needs` has completed
- any approval or guard condition is satisfied
- any loop or branch controller allows the step to proceed
- concurrency limits permit it to run

Because dependencies are explicit, Smithers does not need to infer readiness from a re-rendered tree.

## Phase 4: Execute Ready Steps

Each ready step runs as a normal `Effect`.

```ts
const fix = $.step("fix", {
  output: Fix,
  needs: { analysis },
  run: ({ analysis }) =>
    Effect.gen(function* () {
      const coder = yield* Coder;
      return yield* coder.fix(analysis);
    }),
});
```

The `run` function receives a typed execution context made from:

- validated workflow input
- resolved `needs` outputs
- Smithers execution metadata such as step id, attempt number, and execution id
- cancellation/timeout signals

The step body can then use the normal Effect toolkit:

- `Effect.gen`
- `Context.Tag`
- `Layer`
- `Schedule`
- logging and tracing
- resource scoping

Smithers should not invent a second effect system inside steps.

## Phase 5: Commit Durable Transitions

When a step finishes, Smithers commits the transition durably:

- persist the output model row
- persist attempt metadata
- persist lifecycle events
- update execution state
- unblock downstream dependents

If a step fails, Smithers persists the failure and consults the retry policy. If the retry policy allows another attempt, the step transitions back to `pending` or `ready`; otherwise it becomes `failed`.

This is where Smithers should lean on durable workflow primitives rather than hand-rolled bookkeeping.

## Determinism

Determinism in the new API comes from explicit structure.

### Stable step identity

Each step has an explicit logical id:

```ts
const analyze = $.step("analyze", { ... });
```

That id becomes the durable identity for persistence, logs, retries, approvals, and resume behavior.

### Explicit edges

Dependencies are declared directly:

```ts
const report = $.step("report", {
  needs: { research },
  run: ({ research }) => ...,
});
```

Smithers does not need to discover hidden dependencies by watching when user code reads from a shared context.

### Explicit control flow

Sequence, parallelism, branching, and looping should be represented as explicit builder nodes. That matters because runtime decisions must survive process restarts.

If a workflow has a review loop, the current iteration belongs in durable state, not in transient JavaScript control flow.

## Retry, Timeout, and Approval Semantics

These policies should map directly onto Effect primitives.

### Retries

Retries belong to `Schedule`, not a Smithers-only integer.

```ts
retry: Schedule.exponential("250 millis").pipe(
  Schedule.intersect(Schedule.recurs(3)),
)
```

That makes retry semantics consistent with the rest of an Effect application.

### Timeouts

Timeouts should accept `DurationInput` and be enforced around the step Effect. A timed-out step is persisted as a failed attempt and fed through the same retry policy.

### Approvals

Approvals should suspend the step durably. The important property is not UI integration, but recoverability: after a restart, the workflow must still know that a decision is pending.

## Persistence Model

Smithers should persist two categories of data:

### Domain outputs

These are your declared `Model`s:

```ts
class Review extends Model.Class<Review>("Review")({
  approved: Schema.Boolean,
  feedback: Schema.String,
}) {}
```

### Workflow metadata

This is owned by Smithers:

- executions
- step attempts
- retry history
- approvals
- loop state
- branch decisions
- event log

The user should not have to add workflow bookkeeping columns to every domain model.

## Resume and Replay

Resuming a workflow should mean:

1. load the execution by id
2. rebuild the graph definition
3. reload persisted execution state
4. continue from unfinished durable nodes

Completed steps should not re-run unless the user explicitly asks for replay or invalidates cached state.

That is the core contract of Smithers: workflow definitions are pure and replayable, but execution history is durable and incremental.

## Dynamic Workflows

Builder-based workflows still support dynamic behavior, but it has to be explicit.

Good examples:

- a branch node that chooses a subgraph from a previous step output
- a loop node that repeats until a persisted condition is met
- a queue or merge gate that limits concurrency durably

The unifying rule is that runtime control-flow decisions must be represented in durable state. They cannot depend on an ephemeral re-render of arbitrary user code.

## Why This Matters

This execution model makes the system easier to reason about:

- graph structure is explicit
- dependencies are typed
- runtime services stay inside Effect
- persistence is durable
- retries and approvals have a stable state machine

That is a better fit for workflow orchestration than reusing a UI renderer as the planner.

## Next Steps

- [Data Model](/concepts/data-model) -- See how `Schema`, `Model`, and workflow metadata fit together.
- [Data Model](/concepts/data-model) -- Understand what data a running step receives.
- [Approvals](/concepts/approvals) -- Design durable approval gates on top of Effect primitives.

---

## Data Model

> How Smithers uses Effect Schema, Effect SQL Model, and internal workflow metadata.
> Source: https://smithers.sh/concepts/data-model

The Smithers data model has three layers:

1. **workflow input** described with `Schema`
2. **domain outputs** described with `Model`
3. **execution metadata** owned by Smithers

Those layers serve different purposes and should stay separate.

## Input Uses `Schema`

Workflow input is the external boundary of the run.

**In TOON:**

```toon
input:
  ticketId: string
  description: string
```


Smithers validates input against this schema before the workflow starts. After validation, steps receive a typed `input` value.

Use `Schema` for input because:

- it defines the runtime contract
- it supports validation and decoding
- it does not imply persistence strategy by itself

## Outputs Use `Model`

Step outputs are domain records that Smithers persists.

**In TOON:**

```toon
steps[1]:
  - id: analyze
    output:
      summary: string
      severity: "low" | "medium" | "high"
```

TOON output schemas compile to `Model.Class` definitions automatically.


TOON output schemas compile to model definitions automatically.

You should not need to add fields like:

- `runId`
- `nodeId`
- `iteration`
- `attempt`
- `approvedAt`

Those belong to Smithers, not to your business data.

## Smithers Owns Workflow Metadata

To run durably, Smithers needs internal state that is distinct from user models:

- execution ids
- step ids
- attempt history
- retry state
- timeout state
- approval state
- loop iteration state
- branch decisions
- event journal

This data should live in framework-managed tables, not inside every output model.

That separation is the main difference from the old API, where the user had to think in terms of `(runId, nodeId, iteration)` rows.

## What Gets Persisted

When a step completes, Smithers should persist two things:

### The domain output

The user-declared `Model` value:

```ts
new Analysis({
  summary: "Token refresh silently fails after the first expiration window.",
  severity: "high",
})
```

### The execution transition

Framework state such as:

- which step produced the row
- which execution it belongs to
- which attempt produced it
- when it was written
- whether it unblocked downstream work

The domain model is for your app. The transition record is for orchestration.

## Step Identity and Output Identity

A workflow can use the same `Model` in multiple places:

```ts
const initialReview = $.step("initial-review", {
  output: Review,
  run: ...,
});

const finalReview = $.step("final-review", {
  output: Review,
  run: ...,
});
```

That means persistence cannot be keyed only by model name. Smithers must track at least:

- execution identity
- step identity
- model identity
- iteration or branch identity when relevant

This is another reason workflow metadata needs its own storage layer.

## Why `Model` Instead of Raw Tables

Using `Model` gives Smithers a better abstraction boundary than exposing raw SQL tables:

- the output type is explicit
- validation and encoding are shared
- repositories and queries can use the same Effect SQL model
- the user stays in domain types instead of persistence plumbing

The workflow definition should talk about `Analysis` and `Fix`, not about SQLite column names.

## Direct SQL Access Still Works

Smithers should not hide the SQL layer. If your app needs repositories or reporting queries, use Effect SQL directly against the same database.

```ts
import * as SqliteClient from "@effect/sql-sqlite-bun/SqliteClient";
import { Layer } from "effect";

const DatabaseLive = SqliteClient.layer({
  filename: "./smithers.db",
});
```

The intended architecture is:

- Smithers orchestrates workflow state
- Effect SQL handles model persistence and queries
- both run on the same SQLite client layer

## Input and Dependency Access Inside Steps

A step does not query tables directly in the normal path. Instead, Smithers resolves declared dependencies and passes them in:

```ts
const report = $.step("report", {
  output: Report,
  needs: { analysis },
  run: ({ input, analysis }) =>
    Effect.succeed(
      new Report({
        title: input.ticketId,
        body: analysis.summary,
        wordCount: 42,
      }),
    ),
});
```

That gives you:

- typed workflow input
- typed dependency outputs
- no raw SQL lookups in orchestration code

If you need ad hoc querying, do it explicitly through a repository or SQL service.

## Loop and Branch State

Loops and branches should persist their control-flow state separately from the domain outputs they produce.

Examples:

- current loop iteration
- whether a branch was taken
- which approval decision unblocked a step
- which queue slot is currently owned

These are orchestration facts, not business facts. They should not leak into your `Model` definitions.

## Migrations

Changing a `Model` is a schema change and should be treated as one.

Typical cases:

- adding a field to `Report`
- changing a field type in `Review`
- splitting one model into two models

Smithers should validate that persisted model schemas still match the workflow definition and either:

- apply a migration, or
- fail fast and require one

The workflow runtime should not silently reinterpret old rows with new model definitions.

## Suggested Mental Model

Use this rule of thumb:

- **`Schema`** answers: what input does this workflow accept?
- **`Model`** answers: what domain value does this step persist?
- **Smithers metadata** answers: what happened while the workflow executed?

If a field only matters to orchestration, it probably does not belong in your domain model.

## Next Steps

- [Execution Model](/concepts/execution-model) -- Learn what data a step receives at runtime.
- [Approvals](/concepts/approvals) -- See how approval state should be modeled durably.
- [Caching](/concepts/caching) -- Understand where reusable outputs fit into the data model.

---

## Approvals

> Model human review as an explicit durable gate in the workflow graph.
> Source: https://smithers.sh/concepts/approvals

Approvals should be explicit workflow nodes, not a boolean flag on an otherwise normal step.

From first principles, an approval is:

- a durable request for a human decision
- a suspended execution state
- an audited decision record
- a dependency that downstream work can wait on

That makes approvals a workflow primitive in their own right.

## Approval as a Node

Instead of:

```ts
// Old shape
needsApproval: true
```

approvals are explicit nodes in JSX and TOON.

**In JSX:**

```tsx
import {
  Approval,
  Sequence,
  Task,
  Workflow,
  approvalDecisionSchema,
  createSmithers,
} from "smithers-orchestrator";
import { z } from "zod";

const { smithers, outputs } = createSmithers({
  approval: approvalDecisionSchema,
  published: z.object({ status: z.string() }),
});

export default smithers((ctx) => {
  const approval = ctx.outputMaybe("approval", { nodeId: "approve-deploy" });

  return (
    <Workflow name="deploy">
      <Sequence>
        <Approval
          id="approve-deploy"
          output={outputs.approval}
          request={{
            title: "Deploy to production?",
            summary: "Human review required before release.",
          }}
          onDeny="continue"
        />

        {approval ? (
          <Task id="record" output={outputs.published}>
            {{ status: approval.approved ? "approved" : "rejected" }}
          </Task>
        ) : null}
      </Sequence>
    </Workflow>
  );
});
```

**In TOON:**

```toon
steps[3]:
  - id: build
    prompt: Build the deployment package.
    output:
      version: string
      commitSha: string

  - kind: approval
    id: approve-deploy
    needs[1]: build
    request:
      title: "Deploy {build.version}?"
      summary: "Commit {build.commitSha} passed all checks."
    onDeny: fail

  - id: deploy
    needs[2]: build,approve-deploy
    prompt: Deploy version {build.version}.
    output:
      url: string
```


This is better for three reasons:

1. the approval is visible in the graph
2. the execution can suspend on a first-class durable node
3. the decision can be reused or branched on explicitly

## What an Approval Produces

An approval gate should resolve to a typed decision object:

```ts
type ApprovalDecision = {
  readonly approved: boolean;
  readonly note: string | null;
  readonly decidedBy: string | null;
  readonly decidedAt: string | null;
};
```

That lets downstream nodes depend on the decision as data, not only as scheduler side effects.

## Lifecycle

An approval node should move through a durable lifecycle:

```txt
pending
  -> requested
  -> waiting-approval
  -> approved | denied
  -> completed | failed | routed
```

More concretely:

1. Smithers reaches the approval node.
2. It persists an approval request record.
3. The execution suspends in a durable waiting state.
4. A human approves or denies the request.
5. The node resolves according to its policy.
6. Downstream nodes become eligible to run.

If the process restarts while waiting, the approval request still exists and the execution can resume later.

## Request Shape

The `request` function should be pure and derived from already-computed workflow data:

```ts
request: ({ build }) => ({
  title: `Deploy ${build.version}?`,
  summary: build.plan,
  metadata: {
    risk: build.risk,
    commitSha: build.commitSha,
  },
})
```

That request is what Smithers persists, displays in UIs, and exposes through CLI or API tooling.

## Denial Policies

An approval gate should make denial behavior explicit.

### `onDeny: "fail"`

The workflow fails when the gate is denied.

This is appropriate for destructive or compliance-sensitive actions.

### `onDeny: "continue"`

The gate resolves to a denial decision and the workflow continues. Downstream logic can branch on that value.

### `onDeny: "skip"`

The protected branch is skipped, but the rest of the workflow continues.

The important part is that denial handling is declared in the workflow, not buried inside scheduler heuristics.

## Branching on Approval

Because approvals are values, not only control flags, you can route explicitly:

```ts
const approval = $.approval("approve-release", {
  needs: { report },
  request: ({ report }) => ({
    title: `Publish ${report.title}?`,
    summary: report.body,
  }),
  onDeny: "continue",
});

return $.match(approval, {
  when: (decision) => decision.approved,
  then: () => $.step("publish", { output: Published, run: ... }),
  else: () => $.step("record-rejection", { output: Rejection, run: ... }),
});
```

This is a better fit for builder-based workflows than implicit hidden states.

## Storage Model

Approval state belongs to Smithers-managed workflow metadata, not to domain models.

Smithers should persist at least:

- approval node id
- execution id
- current status
- request payload
- decision payload
- timestamps
- actor identity

That gives you:

- resumability
- auditability
- UI/API query support
- explicit graph semantics

## Approvals and Effect Primitives

Approvals map naturally onto durable Effect concepts:

- a request record is persisted metadata
- the waiting state is a durable suspension point
- the decision behaves like a durable deferred value

Smithers should compile approval nodes onto durable primitives rather than inventing bespoke in-memory waiting logic.

## Notifications and Automation

Approval creation should emit a durable event so other systems can react:

- send Slack messages
- open a review UI
- create a Linear issue
- notify on-call engineers

The important boundary is:

- Smithers records the approval request durably
- external systems subscribe and notify
- the human decision flows back into the same durable gate

## CLI and API Shape

The control plane should target approval nodes directly.

For example:

```bash
smithers approve workflow.ts --run-id <id> --node-id approve-deploy --note "Ship it"
smithers deny workflow.ts --run-id <id> --node-id approve-deploy --note "Blocked by QA"
```

The exact transport can vary, but the key should be `(runId, nodeId)`, not a hidden internal row id.

## Why Explicit Gates Matter

An explicit approval node is easier to reason about than a property on a task because it makes the workflow honest.

You can see:

- where human intervention is required
- what exactly is being approved
- how denial changes the graph
- what downstream work depends on the decision

That is the right abstraction for a durable workflow system.

## Next Steps

- [Caching](/concepts/caching) -- See how reusable outputs and approval-gated work interact.
- [Execution Model](/concepts/execution-model) -- Understand how suspended approval nodes fit into durable execution.
- [Runtime Events](/runtime/events) -- This page will need to describe approval events in the new model.

---

## Caching

> Reuse step outputs when the same workflow inputs and dependencies appear again.
> Source: https://smithers.sh/concepts/caching

Caching in the builder API should be:

- **per-step**
- **explicit**
- **derived from declared inputs and dependencies**
- **validated against the current output model before reuse**

It should not depend on hidden renderer state or ad hoc prompt hashing.

## Step-Level Caching

Caching belongs on the step that produces reusable work.

**In TOON:**

```toon
steps[1]:
  - id: analyze
    prompt: Analyze {input.description}.
    output:
      summary: string
      severity: "low" | "medium" | "high"
    cache:
      by[2]: input.ticketId,input.description
      version: analysis-v1
```


This says:

- cache `analyze`
- key it from the declared `by` function
- invalidate old entries if the algorithm or provider changes by bumping `version`

That is a better fit for Effect workflows than a global workflow-wide cache switch.

## Default Mental Model

The right mental model is:

> A step is cacheable when it behaves like a pure function of its declared inputs.

If a step depends on:

- `input`
- `needs`
- stable service behavior

then Smithers can safely reuse a previous successful output for the same key.

If a step has hidden side effects or reads mutable external state, it should not be cached by default.

## What Goes Into the Cache Key

The default cache key should be derived from durable workflow structure plus the explicit `cache.by` payload.

Good key components:

- workflow name
- step id
- output model identity
- `cache.version`
- serialized value returned by `cache.by`

For example:

```ts
cache: {
  by: ({ input, analysis }) => ({
    repo: input.repo,
    summary: analysis.summary,
  }),
  version: "report-v2",
}
```

This is better than attempting to hash arbitrary service graphs or opaque runtime objects.

## Why Explicit Keys Matter

Explicit keys make cache behavior reviewable.

You can answer:

- what exact data invalidates this step?
- did the provider/model change?
- does this step depend on hidden filesystem or network state?
- should this cache survive a workflow refactor?

Magic cache keys tend to break trust because users cannot predict when a step will reuse old work.

## Cache Validation

When Smithers finds a cache hit, it should still validate the cached payload against the current output model before reusing it.

That protects against:

- model shape changes
- decoding changes
- old invalid cache entries

If validation fails, Smithers should treat the entry as a miss and compute a fresh value.

## Example

```ts
const summarize = $.step("summarize", {
  output: Summary,
  needs: { analysis },
  cache: {
    by: ({ analysis }) => ({
      summary: analysis.summary,
      severity: analysis.severity,
    }),
    version: "summary-v1",
  },
  run: ({ analysis }) =>
    Effect.succeed(
      new Summary({
        title: "Current Status",
        body: analysis.summary,
      }),
    ),
});
```

If the same `analysis` input appears again with the same cache version, Smithers can reuse the persisted `Summary`.

## What Should Not Be Cached

Caching is a bad fit for steps that are primarily about side effects.

Examples:

- deploy to production
- send email
- mutate a Git branch
- call an external system whose current state matters
- open an approval request

Those should either disable caching entirely or use explicit idempotency semantics separate from normal output caching.

## Caching and Effect Services

Effect services are not part of the cache key automatically.

That is intentional. Service instances are often not serializable or stable.

If service behavior affects the output, encode that in the cache version:

```ts
cache: {
  by: ({ input }) => ({ prompt: input.prompt }),
  version: "anthropic-sonnet-4-2025-02",
}
```

This keeps invalidation under user control.

## Runtime Behavior

With caching enabled on a step, Smithers should:

1. compute the cache key before executing the step
2. look for a previously successful cached output
3. validate the cached payload against the output model
4. if valid, mark the step as completed from cache
5. otherwise run the step normally and persist the fresh output

From the rest of the workflow's perspective, a cache hit behaves the same as a completed upstream step.

## Resume vs Cache

Resuming and caching are related but distinct.

### Resume

Resume reuses outputs from the same execution id.

### Cache

Cache reuses outputs across different executions when the declared cache key matches.

Resume is about durability. Cache is about recomputation.

## Storage

Cached outputs should live in Smithers-managed metadata, keyed by:

- workflow id
- step id
- cache key
- cache version

This metadata belongs to the framework, not to user domain models.

## Suggested Rule

Only cache a step if you would be comfortable describing it as:

> "Given these explicit inputs, I want the same persisted output back."

If that sentence feels false, the step probably should not use caching.

## Next Steps

- [Planner Internals](/concepts/planner-internals) -- See how the workflow graph is planned and scheduled internally.
- [Execution Model](/concepts/execution-model) -- See where cache lookup happens in the durable step lifecycle.
- [Runtime Events](/runtime/events) -- This page will need cache-hit and cache-miss events in the new design.

---

## Planner Internals

> How Smithers compiles a builder graph into an executable durable plan.
> Source: https://smithers.sh/concepts/planner-internals

> **Warning:** This page describes internal architecture. You do not need it to write workflows, but it is useful when designing new primitives or debugging scheduler behavior.


Smithers compiles workflow definitions — whether from TOON files or JSX — into a normalized internal plan and drives execution from durable state transitions.

## Pipeline Overview

```txt
TOON File / JSX
  ->
Workflow Graph
  ->
Normalized Plan
  ->
Durable Execution State
  ->
Ready Step Set
  ->
Effect Execution + Commit
```

The internal architecture should be centered on those phases.

## Stage 1: Builder Capture

The workflow definition captures a typed graph. For example, in TOON:

```toon
name: bugfix
input:
  title: string
steps[3]:
  - id: analyze
    run: "return { summary: input.title };\n"
    output:
      summary: string
  - kind: approval
    id: approve
    request:
      title: "Approve fix for {analyze.summary}?"
    onDeny: fail
  - id: fix
    run: "return { patch: 'fix applied' };\n"
    output:
      patch: string
```

At this point Smithers should have an internal graph with explicit node kinds, stable ids, and typed handles.

Conceptually:

```ts
type WorkflowNode =
  | { kind: "step"; id: string; ... }
  | { kind: "sequence"; children: WorkflowNode[] }
  | { kind: "parallel"; children: WorkflowNode[]; maxConcurrency?: number }
  | { kind: "approval"; id: string; ... }
  | { kind: "match"; id: string; ... }
  | { kind: "loop"; id: string; ... };
```

The important part is that the graph is already explicit before execution begins.

## Stage 2: Normalization

Normalization should:

- resolve step handles to stable internal ids
- flatten `needs` into dependency edges
- assign concurrency groups
- attach output model signatures
- attach retry, timeout, cache, and approval policies
- derive branch and loop controller descriptors

After normalization, every executable node should be schedulable without interpreting user builder code again.

## Compiled Step Descriptor

A normalized step descriptor might look like:

```ts
type CompiledStep = {
  id: string;
  outputModel: string;
  needs: ReadonlyArray<{ name: string; stepId: string }>;
  retryPolicy?: unknown;
  timeout?: unknown;
  cachePolicy?: unknown;
  concurrencyGroup?: string;
  run: unknown;
};
```

The `run` implementation remains a callback, but the rest of the descriptor should be plain data.

That distinction matters because the planner and persistence layer must reason about the workflow even when a step is not currently running.

## Stage 3: Durable Execution State

Smithers then combines the normalized plan with persisted execution state.

That state should include:

- execution record
- node state
- completed outputs
- attempt history
- approval state
- loop state
- branch decisions
- cache hits or invalidations

This is the real source of truth for the scheduler. The workflow graph tells Smithers what is possible; durable state tells Smithers what has already happened.

## Stage 4: Ready-Set Computation

The scheduler walks the normalized plan and computes the current ready set.

A step is ready only when:

- all entries in `needs` are completed
- any enclosing sequence has advanced to it
- any approval or branch controller has resolved in its favor
- any loop state allows the current iteration
- concurrency limits allow admission

This is simpler than manually tracking state because scheduling depends on explicit graph edges.

## Stage 5: Execution

Ready steps are executed by invoking their `run` callback with:

- validated input
- resolved dependency outputs
- execution metadata
- cancellation signal

The planner should not know about provider-specific behavior. It only knows how to supply step inputs and interpret success, failure, retry, suspension, or cancellation.

## Stage 6: Commit and Transition

After a step produces a result, Smithers commits:

- the output model row
- attempt metadata
- lifecycle event records
- state transitions for downstream scheduling

This commit phase should be atomic at the workflow level wherever possible. The goal is that after a crash, Smithers can recover from persisted state without guessing.

## Debugging

For debugging, the most useful artifacts are:

- normalized plan snapshots
- node state tables
- dependency edges
- ready-set explanations
- step transition events

If Smithers exposes those clearly, most workflow bugs become much easier to diagnose.

## Next Steps

- [Runtime Events](/runtime/events) — Planner-level transitions and lifecycle events.
- [runWorkflow](/runtime/run-workflow) — Execution entry points for workflows.

---

## runWorkflow

> Execute a Smithers workflow programmatically and get back a durable RunResult.
> Source: https://smithers.sh/runtime/run-workflow

`runWorkflow` is the primary entry point for executing a workflow. It accepts a compiled `SmithersWorkflow` object and a `RunOptions` bag, drives the render-schedule-execute loop until completion, and returns a `RunResult`.

## Basic Usage

```ts
import { createSmithers, Task, runWorkflow } from "smithers-orchestrator";
import { z } from "zod";

const { Workflow, smithers, outputs } = createSmithers({
  analysis: z.object({ summary: z.string() }),
});

const workflow = smithers((ctx) => (
  <Workflow name="example">
    <Task id="analyze" output={outputs.analysis} agent={myAgent}>
      {`Analyze: ${ctx.input.description}`}
    </Task>
  </Workflow>
));

const result = await runWorkflow(workflow, {
  input: { description: "Auth tokens expire silently" },
});

console.log(result.status); // "finished" | "failed" | "cancelled" | "waiting-approval"
```

## Signature

```ts
function runWorkflow<Schema>(
  workflow: SmithersWorkflow<Schema>,
  opts: RunOptions,
): Promise<RunResult>;
```

## RunOptions

| Field | Type | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| `input` | `Record<string, unknown>` | **(required)** | Input data for the run. Must be a JSON object. In the manual API it should match the `input` table schema; in the schema-driven API it is stored as a JSON payload. The `runId` key is injected automatically. |
| `runId` | `string` | Auto-generated | Provide a deterministic run ID. If omitted, a new unique ID is generated. |
| `resume` | `boolean` | `false` | Resume an existing run instead of starting a new one. Requires `runId`. Skips already-completed tasks and picks up from the last checkpoint. |
| `maxConcurrency` | `number` | `4` | Maximum number of tasks that can execute in parallel at any given time. Also respects per-group limits set by `<Parallel maxConcurrency>`. |
| `onProgress` | `(e: SmithersEvent) => void` | `undefined` | Callback invoked for every lifecycle event. See [Events](/runtime/events). |
| `signal` | `AbortSignal` | `undefined` | Standard `AbortSignal` to cancel the run. When aborted, the run finishes with status `"cancelled"`. |
| `workflowPath` | `string` | `undefined` | Absolute or relative path to the workflow `.tsx` file. Used to resolve the default `rootDir` and stored in the run metadata. |
| `rootDir` | `string` | Workflow file's directory | Root directory for the tool sandbox. All file-system tools (`read`, `edit`, `write`, `bash`, `grep`) are confined to this directory. |
| `logDir` | `string \| null` | `.smithers/executions/<runId>/logs` | Directory for NDJSON event log files. Set to `null` to disable log file output entirely. Relative paths are resolved from `rootDir`. |
| `allowNetwork` | `boolean` | `false` | Whether the `bash` tool is permitted to make network requests. |
| `maxOutputBytes` | `number` | `200000` | Maximum number of bytes a single tool call can return. Output is truncated beyond this limit. |
| `toolTimeoutMs` | `number` | `60000` | Maximum wall-clock time (in milliseconds) for a single tool call before it is killed. |
| `hot` | `boolean \| HotReloadOptions` | `undefined` | Enable hot-reload mode. Pass `true` for defaults or a `HotReloadOptions` object to customize. See below. |

### HotReloadOptions

When `hot` is set to a `HotReloadOptions` object, the following sub-options are available:

| Field | Type | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| `rootDir` | `string` | Auto-detect from workflow entry | Root directory to watch for file changes. |
| `outDir` | `string` | `.smithers/hmr/<runId>` | Directory for generation overlays. |
| `maxGenerations` | `number` | `3` | Maximum number of overlay generations to keep. |
| `cancelUnmounted` | `boolean` | `false` | Whether to cancel tasks that become unmounted after a hot reload. |
| `debounceMs` | `number` | `100` | Debounce interval in milliseconds for file change events. |

## RunResult

```ts
type RunResult = {
  runId: string;
  status: "finished" | "failed" | "cancelled" | "waiting-approval";
  output?: unknown;
  error?: unknown;
};
```

| Field | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `runId` | `string` | The run identifier (either provided or auto-generated). |
| `status` | `string` | Terminal status of the run. |
| `output` | `unknown` | Rows from the `output` table in the schema, if the workflow finished successfully and the schema includes an `output` table. See note below. |
| `error` | `unknown` | Error information if the run failed. Contains a serialized error object with `code`, `message`, and optional `details`. |

### Understanding `result.output`

The `output` field on `RunResult` is **only** populated if your schema passed to `createSmithers()` includes a key literally named `output`. For example:

```ts
// ✅ result.output WILL be populated
const { Workflow, smithers, outputs } = createSmithers({
  output: z.object({ summary: z.string() }),
});
```

```ts
// ❌ result.output will be undefined
const { Workflow, smithers, outputs } = createSmithers({
  page: z.object({ title: z.string(), html: z.string() }),
});
```

If your schema uses other key names (like `page`, `analysis`, `research`, etc.), all task outputs are still persisted to SQLite — they're just not returned on `result.output`. To access them programmatically, query the SQLite database directly:

```ts
import { Database } from "bun:sqlite";

const result = await runWorkflow(workflow, { input: {} });
const db = new Database("smithers.db", { readonly: true });
const rows = db.query(
  "SELECT * FROM page WHERE run_id = ? ORDER BY iteration DESC"
).all(result.runId);
db.close();
```

Alternatively, name your schema key `output` if you want the convenience of `result.output`.

### Status Values

| Status | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `"finished"` | All tasks completed successfully. |
| `"failed"` | A task failed (after exhausting retries) and `continueOnFail` was not set. |
| `"cancelled"` | The run was cancelled via `AbortSignal`. |
| `"waiting-approval"` | At least one task requires human approval before the run can continue. Use `smithers approve` or `smithers deny` to unblock. |

## Resuming a Run

To resume a previously paused or crashed run, pass `resume: true` along with the original `runId`. Smithers reads the persisted state from SQLite, skips tasks that already have valid output rows, and continues executing from the first pending task.

```ts
const result = await runWorkflow(workflow, {
  input: {},
  runId: "my-run-123",
  resume: true,
});
```

When resuming:

- The original input row is loaded from the database. You do not need to provide `input` data again (pass an empty object).
- The stored workflow path, workflow file hash, and VCS metadata must still match the current environment.
- Stale in-progress attempts older than 15 minutes are automatically cancelled and retried.
- Tasks with valid persisted outputs are skipped.

## Cancellation

Pass an `AbortSignal` to cancel a running workflow:

```ts
const controller = new AbortController();

// Cancel after 5 minutes
setTimeout(() => controller.abort(), 5 * 60 * 1000);

const result = await runWorkflow(workflow, {
  input: { description: "Long task" },
  signal: controller.signal,
});

if (result.status === "cancelled") {
  console.log("Run was cancelled");
}
```

When cancelled, all in-progress task attempts are marked as cancelled in the database and `NodeCancelled` events are emitted.

## Event Monitoring

Use the `onProgress` callback to receive real-time events as the run progresses:

```ts
const result = await runWorkflow(workflow, {
  input: { description: "Fix bug" },
  onProgress: (event) => {
    switch (event.type) {
      case "NodeStarted":
        console.log(`Task ${event.nodeId} started (attempt ${event.attempt})`);
        break;
      case "NodeFinished":
        console.log(`Task ${event.nodeId} finished`);
        break;
      case "NodeFailed":
        console.error(`Task ${event.nodeId} failed:`, event.error);
        break;
      case "ApprovalRequested":
        console.log(`Task ${event.nodeId} needs approval`);
        break;
    }
  },
});
```

See [Events](/runtime/events) for the full list of event types and their fields.

## Idle Sleep Prevention

On macOS, `runWorkflow` automatically acquires a `caffeinate` lock to prevent the system from idle-sleeping while a workflow is running. The lock is released when the run finishes, fails, or is cancelled. This is a no-op on other platforms.

## Error Handling

If an unhandled exception occurs inside the engine (outside of individual task execution), the run is marked as `"failed"` and the error is serialized into `RunResult.error`. Individual task failures are handled by the retry and `continueOnFail` mechanisms.

Set `SMITHERS_DEBUG=1` in your environment to print engine-level errors to stderr.

## Related

- [Events](/runtime/events) -- All event types emitted during a run.
- [renderFrame](/runtime/render-frame) -- Preview the workflow graph without executing.
- [CLI](/cli/overview) -- Run workflows from the command line.
- [Resumability](/guides/resumability) -- How durable state and crash recovery work.

---

## renderFrame

> Render a workflow tree to a GraphSnapshot for visualization and debugging without executing any tasks.
> Source: https://smithers.sh/runtime/render-frame

`renderFrame` is a pure rendering function that converts a workflow's JSX tree into a `GraphSnapshot` containing an XML representation and an ordered list of task descriptors. It does not execute any tasks, call any agents, or modify the database. This makes it useful for visualization, debugging, and building workflow inspectors.

## Basic Usage

```ts
import { renderFrame } from "smithers-orchestrator";
import workflow from "./workflow";

const snapshot = await renderFrame(workflow, {
  runId: "preview",
  iteration: 0,
  input: { description: "Fix authentication bug" },
  outputs: {},
});

console.log(snapshot.frameNo);       // 0
console.log(snapshot.tasks.length);  // Number of tasks in the tree
console.log(snapshot.xml);           // XML tree representation
```

## Signature

```ts
function renderFrame<Schema>(
  workflow: SmithersWorkflow<Schema>,
  ctx: {
    runId: string;
    iteration: number;
    iterations?: Record<string, number>;
    input: object;
    outputs: object;
  },
  opts?: { baseRootDir?: string },
): Promise<GraphSnapshot>;
```

### Context Object

The second argument is a plain object that provides the data your workflow's JSX tree needs to render. It mirrors the `SmithersCtx` that `runWorkflow` builds internally. The parameter is typed as `any` in source, but it expects the shape shown above.

| Field | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `runId` | `string` | The run ID to use in the snapshot. Can be any string for preview purposes. |
| `iteration` | `number` | The current loop iteration number (use `0` for non-looping workflows). |
| `iterations` | `Record<string, number>` | Per-loop iteration counts, keyed by loop ID. Optional. |
| `input` | `object` | The input data the workflow expects from `ctx.input`. |
| `outputs` | `object` | Previously computed outputs, keyed by table name. Pass `{}` to render the initial state. |

### Options (third argument)

| Field | Type | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| `baseRootDir` | `string` | `undefined` | Base root directory used to resolve relative paths inside the workflow tree. |

## GraphSnapshot

```ts
type GraphSnapshot = {
  runId: string;
  frameNo: number;
  xml: XmlNode | null;
  tasks: TaskDescriptor[];
};
```

| Field | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `runId` | `string` | The run ID from the context. |
| `frameNo` | `number` | Always `0` for `renderFrame` (frame numbering is managed by the engine during actual execution). |
| `xml` | `XmlNode \| null` | The rendered XML tree, or `null` if the workflow produced no output. |
| `tasks` | `TaskDescriptor[]` | Ordered list of all `<Task>` nodes found in the tree, in execution order. |

### XmlNode

The XML tree is a recursive structure of elements and text nodes:

```ts
type XmlNode = XmlElement | XmlText;

type XmlElement = {
  kind: "element";
  tag: string;           // e.g. "smithers:workflow", "smithers:task"
  props: Record<string, string>;
  children: XmlNode[];
};

type XmlText = {
  kind: "text";
  text: string;
};
```

### TaskDescriptor

Each task in the tree produces a `TaskDescriptor` that describes its configuration:

```ts
type TaskDescriptor = {
  nodeId: string;
  ordinal: number;
  iteration: number;
  ralphId?: string;
  dependsOn?: string[];
  needs?: Record<string, string>;
  worktreeId?: string;
  worktreePath?: string;
  worktreeBranch?: string;

  outputTable: Table | null;
  outputTableName: string;
  outputRef?: ZodObject<any>;
  outputSchema?: ZodObject<any>;

  parallelGroupId?: string;
  parallelMaxConcurrency?: number;

  needsApproval: boolean;
  approvalMode?: "gate" | "decision";
  approvalOnDeny?: "fail" | "continue" | "skip";
  skipIf: boolean;
  retries: number;
  retryPolicy?: RetryPolicy;
  timeoutMs: number | null;
  continueOnFail: boolean;
  cachePolicy?: CachePolicy;

  agent?: AgentLike | AgentLike[];
  prompt?: string;
  staticPayload?: unknown;
  computeFn?: () => unknown | Promise<unknown>;

  label?: string;
  meta?: Record<string, unknown>;
};
```

| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| `nodeId` | The `id` prop from the `<Task>` component. |
| `ordinal` | Position in the task list (0-indexed). |
| `iteration` | The loop iteration this task belongs to. |
| `ralphId` | The ID of the enclosing `<Loop>` component, if any. |
| `dependsOn` | Array of node IDs this task explicitly depends on. |
| `needs` | Named dependencies. Keys become context keys, values are task node IDs. |
| `worktreeId` | ID of the git worktree assigned to this task. |
| `worktreePath` | Filesystem path to the git worktree. |
| `worktreeBranch` | Branch name for the git worktree. |
| `outputTable` | The Drizzle table object for the task's output. |
| `outputTableName` | String name of the output table. |
| `outputRef` | Zod schema reference from the `<Task>` `output` prop (used for schema resolution). |
| `outputSchema` | Optional Zod schema for validating agent output. |
| `parallelGroupId` | ID of the `<Parallel>` group this task belongs to. |
| `parallelMaxConcurrency` | Per-group concurrency limit from the enclosing `<Parallel>`. |
| `needsApproval` | Whether this task requires human approval before executing. |
| `approvalMode` | Approval style: `"gate"` pauses before execution, `"decision"` records a decision. |
| `approvalOnDeny` | Behavior when approval is denied: `"fail"`, `"continue"`, or `"skip"`. |
| `skipIf` | Whether this task should be skipped. |
| `retries` | Number of retry attempts on failure. |
| `retryPolicy` | Retry backoff configuration (`{ backoff?, initialDelayMs? }`). |
| `timeoutMs` | Per-task timeout in milliseconds, or `null` for the global default. |
| `continueOnFail` | Whether the workflow continues if this task fails. |
| `cachePolicy` | Cache configuration (`{ by?, version? }`). |
| `agent` | The AI agent (or array of agents for fallback) assigned to this task. |
| `prompt` | The resolved prompt string (from `<Task>` children). |
| `staticPayload` | Static output data (for tasks without an agent). |
| `computeFn` | Callback function invoked at execution time (for compute tasks). |
| `label` | Human-readable label for display. |
| `meta` | Arbitrary metadata attached to the task. |

## Use Cases

### Previewing the Execution Graph

Render a workflow to see what tasks will run before actually executing them:

```ts
const snapshot = await renderFrame(workflow, {
  runId: "dry-run",
  iteration: 0,
  input: { description: "Preview" },
  outputs: {},
});

for (const task of snapshot.tasks) {
  console.log(`${task.ordinal}. [${task.nodeId}] -> ${task.outputTableName}`);
  if (task.needsApproval) console.log("   (requires approval)");
  if (task.skipIf) console.log("   (skipped)");
}
```

### Simulating Completed Outputs

Pass previously computed outputs to see how the tree changes:

```ts
// First render: no outputs
const snap1 = await renderFrame(workflow, {
  runId: "sim",
  iteration: 0,
  input: { description: "Bug" },
  outputs: {},
});

// Second render: simulate "analyze" completing
const snap2 = await renderFrame(workflow, {
  runId: "sim",
  iteration: 0,
  input: { description: "Bug" },
  outputs: {
    analyze: [{ runId: "sim", nodeId: "analyze", iteration: 0, summary: "Found null pointer" }],
  },
});
```

### Building a Workflow Visualizer

The XML tree and task list can be used to build visual representations of the workflow DAG. The `xml` field mirrors the JSX structure, while `tasks` provides the flattened execution order.

### CLI Graph Command

The `smithers graph` CLI command uses `renderFrame` internally:

```bash
smithers graph workflow.tsx --input '{"description": "Fix bug"}'
```

This prints the `GraphSnapshot` as JSON to stdout.

## Related

- [runWorkflow](/runtime/run-workflow) -- Execute the workflow for real.
- [Events](/runtime/events) -- Monitor execution progress.
- [Execution Model](/concepts/execution-model) -- How the render-execute-persist loop works.

---

## Events

> Subscribe to fine-grained lifecycle events emitted during workflow execution.
> Source: https://smithers.sh/runtime/events

Smithers emits typed `SmithersEvent` objects throughout a run's lifecycle. You can subscribe via the `onProgress` callback in `runWorkflow`, or read persisted events from the NDJSON log files after the fact.

Those events are also part of the observability pipeline:

- they remain the durable replay and audit log for each run
- they correlate with structured logs through shared fields like `runId`, `nodeId`, and `attempt`
- they drive Smithers' built-in lifecycle counters and gauges

For OTLP export, Prometheus/Grafana setup, and the public observability service tag, see [Observability](/guides/monitoring-logs).

## Subscribing to Events

### onProgress Callback

Pass an `onProgress` function to `runWorkflow` to receive events in real time:

```ts
import { runWorkflow } from "smithers-orchestrator";
import workflow from "./workflow";

await runWorkflow(workflow, {
  input: { description: "Fix bug" },
  onProgress: (event) => {
    console.log(`[${event.type}] at ${event.timestampMs}`);

    if (event.type === "NodeStarted") {
      console.log(`  node: ${event.nodeId}, attempt: ${event.attempt}`);
    }

    if (event.type === "NodeFailed") {
      console.error(`  node: ${event.nodeId}, error:`, event.error);
    }
  },
});
```

### NDJSON Log Files

When logging is enabled (the default), every event is appended as a JSON line to:

```
.smithers/executions/<runId>/logs/stream.ndjson
```

Each line is a complete `SmithersEvent` object serialized as JSON. You can parse these files with standard tools:

```bash
# Watch events in real time
tail -f .smithers/executions/abc123/logs/stream.ndjson | jq .

# Filter for failures
cat .smithers/executions/abc123/logs/stream.ndjson | jq 'select(.type == "NodeFailed")'

# Count events by type
cat .smithers/executions/abc123/logs/stream.ndjson | jq -r .type | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn
```

To change the log directory, pass `logDir` to `runWorkflow` or `--log-dir` to the CLI. To disable log files entirely, pass `logDir: null` or `--no-log`.

## Event-Driven Metrics

Smithers maps key event types onto built-in runtime metrics. For example:

- `RunStarted` increments `smithers.runs.total`
- `NodeStarted` increments `smithers.nodes.started`
- `NodeFinished` increments `smithers.nodes.finished`
- `NodeFailed` increments `smithers.nodes.failed`
- approval events increment the approval counters

That mapping is exposed as `trackSmithersEvent` from `smithers-orchestrator/observability` for advanced integrations, but most applications should rely on the built-in runtime wiring.

## Common Fields

Every `SmithersEvent` includes:

| Field | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `type` | `string` | The event type discriminator (e.g. `"NodeStarted"`, `"RunFinished"`). |
| `runId` | `string` | The run this event belongs to. |
| `timestampMs` | `number` | Unix timestamp in milliseconds when the event was emitted. |

Node-scoped events additionally include:

| Field | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `nodeId` | `string` | The task node this event relates to. |
| `iteration` | `number` | The loop iteration number. |

Attempt-scoped events additionally include:

| Field | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `attempt` | `number` | The attempt number (starts at 1, increments on retry). |

## Event Types

### Run Lifecycle

These events mark the overall run's state transitions.

#### RunStarted

Emitted once at the beginning of every run (including resumes).

```ts
{ type: "RunStarted", runId: string, timestampMs: number }
```

#### RunStatusChanged

Emitted when the run's status changes (e.g. from `"running"` to `"waiting-approval"`).

```ts
{ type: "RunStatusChanged", runId: string, status: RunStatus, timestampMs: number }
```

`RunStatus` is one of: `"running"`, `"waiting-approval"`, `"finished"`, `"failed"`, `"cancelled"`.

#### RunFinished

Emitted when all tasks complete successfully.

```ts
{ type: "RunFinished", runId: string, timestampMs: number }
```

#### RunFailed

Emitted when the run fails due to a task failure or engine error.

```ts
{ type: "RunFailed", runId: string, error: unknown, timestampMs: number }
```

#### RunCancelled

Emitted when the run is cancelled via `AbortSignal`.

```ts
{ type: "RunCancelled", runId: string, timestampMs: number }
```

### Frame Events

#### FrameCommitted

Emitted each time the engine renders a new frame (a snapshot of the workflow tree).

```ts
{
  type: "FrameCommitted",
  runId: string,
  frameNo: number,
  xmlHash: string,
  timestampMs: number,
}
```

The `xmlHash` is a SHA-256 hex digest of the canonicalized XML tree. It changes whenever the tree structure changes (e.g. new tasks appear after a Branch resolves).

### Node Lifecycle

These events track individual task execution.

#### NodePending

A task has been identified in the tree and is waiting to be scheduled.

```ts
{ type: "NodePending", runId: string, nodeId: string, iteration: number, timestampMs: number }
```

#### NodeStarted

A task has begun executing. The `attempt` number starts at 1 and increments on each retry.

```ts
{
  type: "NodeStarted",
  runId: string,
  nodeId: string,
  iteration: number,
  attempt: number,
  timestampMs: number,
}
```

#### NodeFinished

A task completed successfully and its output was persisted.

```ts
{
  type: "NodeFinished",
  runId: string,
  nodeId: string,
  iteration: number,
  attempt: number,
  timestampMs: number,
}
```

#### NodeFailed

A task attempt failed. Check the `error` field for details.

```ts
{
  type: "NodeFailed",
  runId: string,
  nodeId: string,
  iteration: number,
  attempt: number,
  error: unknown,
  timestampMs: number,
}
```

#### NodeCancelled

A task was cancelled (e.g. because the run was aborted or the task was unmounted from the tree between frames).

```ts
{
  type: "NodeCancelled",
  runId: string,
  nodeId: string,
  iteration: number,
  attempt?: number,
  reason?: string,
  timestampMs: number,
}
```

The `reason` field may contain `"unmounted"` if the task was cancelled because it disappeared from the tree after a re-render.

#### NodeSkipped

A task was skipped because its `skipIf` condition evaluated to `true` or its approval was denied with `continueOnFail`.

```ts
{ type: "NodeSkipped", runId: string, nodeId: string, iteration: number, timestampMs: number }
```

#### NodeRetrying

A task failed but has remaining retry attempts. This event fires before the next attempt starts.

```ts
{
  type: "NodeRetrying",
  runId: string,
  nodeId: string,
  iteration: number,
  attempt: number,
  timestampMs: number,
}
```

The `attempt` field is the upcoming attempt number (e.g. `2` for the first retry).

#### NodeWaitingApproval

A task requires human approval and is now paused.

```ts
{
  type: "NodeWaitingApproval",
  runId: string,
  nodeId: string,
  iteration: number,
  timestampMs: number,
}
```

### Approval Events

#### ApprovalRequested

An approval request was created for a task.

```ts
{
  type: "ApprovalRequested",
  runId: string,
  nodeId: string,
  iteration: number,
  timestampMs: number,
}
```

#### ApprovalGranted

A human approved the task. The task will execute on the next engine cycle.

```ts
{
  type: "ApprovalGranted",
  runId: string,
  nodeId: string,
  iteration: number,
  timestampMs: number,
}
```

#### ApprovalDenied

A human denied the task. The task is marked as failed (or skipped if `continueOnFail` is set).

```ts
{
  type: "ApprovalDenied",
  runId: string,
  nodeId: string,
  iteration: number,
  timestampMs: number,
}
```

### Tool Events

#### ToolCallStarted

An agent invoked a tool (e.g. `read`, `bash`, `edit`).

```ts
{
  type: "ToolCallStarted",
  runId: string,
  nodeId: string,
  iteration: number,
  attempt: number,
  toolName: string,
  seq: number,
  timestampMs: number,
}
```

The `seq` field is a sequential counter for tool calls within the attempt.

#### ToolCallFinished

A tool call completed.

```ts
{
  type: "ToolCallFinished",
  runId: string,
  nodeId: string,
  iteration: number,
  attempt: number,
  toolName: string,
  seq: number,
  status: "success" | "error",
  timestampMs: number,
}
```

### Output Events

#### NodeOutput

Streaming text output from an agent (stdout or stderr).

```ts
{
  type: "NodeOutput",
  runId: string,
  nodeId: string,
  iteration: number,
  attempt: number,
  text: string,
  stream: "stdout" | "stderr",
  timestampMs: number,
}
```

### Revert Events

#### RevertStarted

A workspace revert operation has begun.

```ts
{
  type: "RevertStarted",
  runId: string,
  nodeId: string,
  iteration: number,
  attempt: number,
  jjPointer: string,
  timestampMs: number,
}
```

#### RevertFinished

A workspace revert operation completed.

```ts
{
  type: "RevertFinished",
  runId: string,
  nodeId: string,
  iteration: number,
  attempt: number,
  jjPointer: string,
  success: boolean,
  error?: string,
  timestampMs: number,
}
```

### Hot Reload

These events are emitted when [hot module replacement](/guides/hot-reload) is enabled.

#### WorkflowReloadDetected

Emitted when file changes are detected in the watched source tree.

```ts
{
  type: "WorkflowReloadDetected",
  runId: string,
  changedFiles: string[],
  timestampMs: number
}
```

#### WorkflowReloaded

Emitted when the workflow module has been successfully reloaded.

```ts
{
  type: "WorkflowReloaded",
  runId: string,
  generation: number,
  changedFiles: string[],
  timestampMs: number
}
```

`generation` is a monotonically increasing counter that tracks how many successful reloads have occurred during this run.

#### WorkflowReloadFailed

Emitted when a reload attempt fails (syntax error, import error, runtime error at module scope).

```ts
{
  type: "WorkflowReloadFailed",
  runId: string,
  error: unknown,
  changedFiles: string[],
  timestampMs: number
}
```

The engine continues running with the previous valid workflow code. Fix the error and save again to retry.

#### WorkflowReloadUnsafe

Emitted when a reload is blocked because it would change the database schema.

```ts
{
  type: "WorkflowReloadUnsafe",
  runId: string,
  reason: string,
  changedFiles: string[],
  timestampMs: number
}
```

Schema changes (adding/removing output schemas, changing Zod shapes, changing `dbPath`) require a process restart.

## Event Type Reference

Quick reference table of all event types and their extra fields (beyond `type`, `runId`, `timestampMs`):

| Event Type | Extra Fields |
|---|---|
| `RunStarted` | -- |
| `RunStatusChanged` | `status` |
| `RunFinished` | -- |
| `RunFailed` | `error` |
| `RunCancelled` | -- |
| `FrameCommitted` | `frameNo`, `xmlHash` |
| `NodePending` | `nodeId`, `iteration` |
| `NodeStarted` | `nodeId`, `iteration`, `attempt` |
| `NodeFinished` | `nodeId`, `iteration`, `attempt` |
| `NodeFailed` | `nodeId`, `iteration`, `attempt`, `error` |
| `NodeCancelled` | `nodeId`, `iteration`, `attempt?`, `reason?` |
| `NodeSkipped` | `nodeId`, `iteration` |
| `NodeRetrying` | `nodeId`, `iteration`, `attempt` |
| `NodeWaitingApproval` | `nodeId`, `iteration` |
| `ApprovalRequested` | `nodeId`, `iteration` |
| `ApprovalGranted` | `nodeId`, `iteration` |
| `ApprovalDenied` | `nodeId`, `iteration` |
| `ToolCallStarted` | `nodeId`, `iteration`, `attempt`, `toolName`, `seq` |
| `ToolCallFinished` | `nodeId`, `iteration`, `attempt`, `toolName`, `seq`, `status` |
| `NodeOutput` | `nodeId`, `iteration`, `attempt`, `text`, `stream` |
| `RevertStarted` | `nodeId`, `iteration`, `attempt`, `jjPointer` |
| `RevertFinished` | `nodeId`, `iteration`, `attempt`, `jjPointer`, `success`, `error?` |
| `WorkflowReloadDetected` | `changedFiles` |
| `WorkflowReloaded` | `generation`, `changedFiles` |
| `WorkflowReloadFailed` | `error`, `changedFiles` |
| `WorkflowReloadUnsafe` | `reason`, `changedFiles` |

## Persistence

Events are persisted in two places:

1. **SQLite database** -- Every event is inserted into the `_smithers_events` table with a sequential `seq` number, enabling replay and querying.
2. **NDJSON log file** -- Each event is appended to `stream.ndjson` in the run's log directory on a best-effort basis. SQLite remains the source of truth if file logging fails.

Both persistence mechanisms happen asynchronously. The `onProgress` callback fires synchronously on the event bus before persistence.

## Related

- [runWorkflow](/runtime/run-workflow) -- Where `onProgress` is configured.
- [Monitoring and Logs](/guides/monitoring-logs) -- Practical guide to monitoring workflows.
- [CLI](/cli/overview) -- View run status and frames from the command line.

---

## Revert to Attempt

> Rewind the workspace to a previous task attempt's state using JJ (Jujutsu) snapshots.
> Source: https://smithers.sh/runtime/revert

Smithers records a [JJ (Jujutsu)](https://jj-vcs.github.io/jj/) change ID at the end of each successful task attempt. You can use the revert feature to restore the workspace to the exact filesystem state that existed when a specific attempt completed. This is useful for debugging, undoing agent changes, or replaying a task from a known-good state.

## Prerequisites

- **JJ must be installed** and available in your `PATH`. Install it with `brew install jj`, `cargo install jj-cli`, or see the [JJ installation docs](https://jj-vcs.github.io/jj/latest/install-and-setup/).
- The workspace must be a JJ repository (run `jj git init` or `jj init` in your project root).
- The attempt you want to revert to must have a recorded JJ pointer. Pointers are only recorded when JJ is available at the time the attempt finishes.

## How It Works

1. When a task attempt finishes successfully, Smithers runs `jj log -r @ --no-graph --template change_id` to capture the current JJ change ID.
2. This change ID is stored in the `_smithers_attempts` table in the `jj_pointer` column.
3. When you invoke `smithers revert`, Smithers looks up the stored change ID and runs `jj restore --from <change_id>` to restore the workspace to that state.

The revert operation restores file contents but does not alter the JJ history. It creates a new change on top of the current working copy with the restored content.

## CLI Usage

```bash
smithers revert <workflow.tsx> \
  --run-id <run-id> \
  --node-id <node-id> \
  [--attempt N] \
  [--iteration N]
```

### Required Flags

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `--run-id ID` | The run containing the attempt to revert to. |
| `--node-id ID` | The task node whose attempt you want to revert to. |

### Optional Flags

| Flag | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `--attempt N` | `1` | The attempt number to revert to (1-indexed). |
| `--iteration N` | `0` | The loop iteration number. |

### Examples

Revert to the first attempt of task "analyze" in a specific run:

```bash
smithers revert workflow.tsx --run-id abc123 --node-id analyze
```

Revert to the second attempt (after a retry):

```bash
smithers revert workflow.tsx --run-id abc123 --node-id analyze --attempt 2
```

Revert to a specific loop iteration:

```bash
smithers revert workflow.tsx --run-id abc123 --node-id fix --attempt 1 --iteration 2
```

### Exit Codes

| Code | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `0` | Revert succeeded. |
| `1` | Revert failed (attempt not found, no JJ pointer, or JJ command failed). |

### Output

The command prints a JSON result to stdout:

```json
{
  "success": true,
  "jjPointer": "zxkqmrstvwxy"
}
```

On failure:

```json
{
  "success": false,
  "error": "Attempt has no jjPointer recorded",
  "jjPointer": null
}
```

During execution, `RevertStarted` and `RevertFinished` events are printed as JSON lines to stdout.

## Programmatic Usage

You can also revert programmatically using the `revertToAttempt` function:

```ts
import { SmithersDb, ensureSmithersTables, revertToAttempt } from "smithers-orchestrator";

const adapter = new SmithersDb(db);

const result = await revertToAttempt(adapter, {
  runId: "abc123",
  nodeId: "analyze",
  iteration: 0,
  attempt: 1,
  onProgress: (event) => console.log(event.type),
});

if (result.success) {
  console.log("Reverted to", result.jjPointer);
} else {
  console.error("Revert failed:", result.error);
}
```

### RevertOptions

```ts
type RevertOptions = {
  runId: string;
  nodeId: string;
  iteration: number;
  attempt: number;
  onProgress?: (event: SmithersEvent) => void;
};
```

### RevertResult

```ts
type RevertResult = {
  success: boolean;
  error?: string;
  jjPointer?: string;
};
```

## Events

Revert operations emit two events:

- **`RevertStarted`** -- Fired before the `jj restore` command runs. Includes the `jjPointer` being restored to.
- **`RevertFinished`** -- Fired after the restore completes. Includes `success` (boolean) and `error` (string, if failed).

See [Events](/runtime/events#revert-events) for the full type definitions.

## Troubleshooting

**"Attempt has no jjPointer recorded"**

The attempt finished before JJ was installed, or JJ was not available in `PATH` when the attempt completed. JJ pointers are only captured opportunistically -- if `jj log` fails, the pointer is silently set to `null`.

**"jj exited with code 1"**

The JJ restore command failed. Common causes:
- The change ID no longer exists in the JJ repository (pruned or garbage-collected).
- The workspace is in a conflicted state.
- JJ is not properly configured for the repository.

**Revert does not undo database changes**

Revert only restores the filesystem via JJ. It does not modify the SQLite database. Task outputs, attempt records, and run state remain unchanged. To re-run a task, resume the workflow -- Smithers will detect that the output is now invalid and re-execute.

## Related

- [Events](/runtime/events) -- RevertStarted and RevertFinished event types.
- [VCS Integration](/guides/vcs) -- How Smithers integrates with version control.
- [CLI](/cli/overview) -- Full CLI reference including the revert command.

---

## CLI Reference

> Complete reference for the smithers command-line interface -- run, resume, approve, inspect, and revert workflows.
> Source: https://smithers.sh/cli/overview

The `smithers` CLI ships with the `smithers-orchestrator` package. It provides commands to run workflows, resume paused runs, manage approvals, inspect state, and revert to previous snapshots.

```bash
bunx smithers <command> [options]
```

## Commands

### run

Execute a workflow from a `.tsx` or `.toon` file.

```bash
smithers run <workflow.tsx | workflow.toon> [options]
```

| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| `--input JSON` | Input data as a JSON string. Parsed and passed to `runWorkflow`. |
| `--run-id ID` | Provide a specific run ID. Must not already exist (use `resume` for existing runs). |
| `--resume` | Resume a previous run instead of starting fresh. Requires `--run-id`. This is an alternative to the standalone `smithers resume` command. |
| `--max-concurrency N` | Maximum parallel tasks. Default: `4`. |
| `--root PATH` | Root directory for the tool sandbox. Default: the workflow file's directory. |
| `--log-dir PATH` | Directory for NDJSON event logs. Relative to root. Default: `.smithers/executions/<runId>/logs`. |
| `--no-log` | Disable event log file output entirely. |
| `--allow-network` | Allow the built-in `bash` tool to make network requests. **Note:** This flag only applies to Smithers' built-in tools. CLI agent wrappers (`ClaudeCodeAgent`, `CodexAgent`, `GeminiAgent`) manage their own sandboxing independently -- use the agent's own sandbox options (e.g. `sandbox: "read-only"` for CodexAgent) to control their network access. |
| `--max-output-bytes N` | Maximum bytes a single tool call can return. Default: `200000`. |
| `--tool-timeout-ms N` | Maximum wall-clock time per tool call in milliseconds. Default: `60000`. |
| `--hot` | Enable hot module replacement. Watches workflow source files and reloads on change without restarting. See [Hot Reload](/guides/hot-reload). |

**Example:**

```bash
smithers run workflow.tsx --input '{"description": "Fix auth bug"}' --max-concurrency 2
```

The command prints the `RunResult` as JSON to stdout and exits with the appropriate [exit code](#exit-codes).

---

### resume

Resume a paused or crashed run. Smithers reloads persisted state from SQLite and continues from the last checkpoint.

```bash
smithers resume <workflow.tsx | workflow.toon> --run-id ID [options]
```

Accepts the same options as `run`. Use `--hot` to enable hot reload during resumed runs.

| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| `--run-id ID` | **(required)** Run ID to resume. |
| `--input JSON` | Input data as a JSON string. Overrides the input persisted in the database. |
| `--force` | Resume even if the run is still marked as running. Without this flag, resuming a run with status `"running"` will fail. |

**Example:**

```bash
smithers resume workflow.tsx --run-id abc123
```

Force-resume a run that is still marked as running (e.g. after a crash):

```bash
smithers resume workflow.tsx --run-id abc123 --force
```

---

### approve

Grant approval for a task that has `needsApproval` set. The workflow must be in `waiting-approval` status.

```bash
smithers approve <workflow.tsx> --run-id ID --node-id ID [options]
```

| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| `--iteration N` | Loop iteration number. Default: `0`. |
| `--note TEXT` | Optional note to attach to the approval record. |
| `--decided-by TEXT` | Name or identifier of the person approving. |

**Example:**

```bash
smithers approve workflow.tsx --run-id abc123 --node-id deploy --note "Reviewed output" --decided-by alice
```

After approving, resume the workflow to continue execution:

```bash
smithers resume workflow.tsx --run-id abc123
```

---

### deny

Deny approval for a task. The task will be marked as failed (or skipped if `continueOnFail` is set on the task).

```bash
smithers deny <workflow.tsx> --run-id ID --node-id ID [options]
```

| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| `--iteration N` | Loop iteration number. Default: `0`. |
| `--note TEXT` | Optional note explaining the denial. |
| `--decided-by TEXT` | Name or identifier of the person denying. |

**Example:**

```bash
smithers deny workflow.tsx --run-id abc123 --node-id deploy --note "Output looks wrong" --decided-by bob
```

---

### status

Print the current status of a run as JSON.

```bash
smithers status <workflow.tsx> --run-id ID
```

**Example:**

```bash
smithers status workflow.tsx --run-id abc123
```

Output:

```json
{
  "runId": "abc123",
  "workflowName": "bugfix",
  "status": "waiting-approval",
  "createdAtMs": 1707500000000,
  "startedAtMs": 1707500000100,
  "finishedAtMs": null
}
```

---

### frames

List the render frames for a run. Frames capture the workflow tree's state at each render cycle.

```bash
smithers frames <workflow.tsx> --run-id ID [--tail N] [--compact]
```

| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| `--tail N` | Number of most recent frames to return. Default: `20`. |
| `--compact` | Omit the full XML tree and show only task states, frame number, and mounted task IDs. Much smaller output for large workflows. |

**Example:**

```bash
smithers frames workflow.tsx --run-id abc123 --tail 5
```

---

### list

List all runs stored in the workflow's database.

```bash
smithers list <workflow.tsx> [--limit N] [--status STATUS]
```

| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| `--limit N` | Maximum number of runs to return. Default: `50`. |
| `--status STATUS` | Filter by status: `running`, `finished`, `failed`, `cancelled`, `waiting-approval`. |

**Example:**

```bash
smithers list workflow.tsx --status failed --limit 10
```

---

### graph

Render the workflow tree without executing, and print the `GraphSnapshot` as JSON. Uses [renderFrame](/runtime/render-frame) internally.

```bash
smithers graph <workflow.tsx> [--run-id ID] [--input JSON]
```

| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| `--run-id ID` | Run ID to use in the context. Default: `"graph"`. If an existing run ID is provided, its persisted input and outputs are loaded. |
| `--input JSON` | Input data as JSON. Overrides the persisted input if both are provided. |

**Example:**

```bash
smithers graph workflow.tsx --input '{"description": "Preview"}'
```

This is useful for understanding the workflow structure, debugging conditional branches, and building external tooling.

---

### revert

Revert the workspace to a previous task attempt's filesystem state using JJ (Jujutsu). See [Revert](/runtime/revert) for full details.

```bash
smithers revert <workflow.tsx> --run-id ID --node-id ID [--attempt N] [--iteration N]
```

| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| `--attempt N` | Attempt number to revert to. Default: `1`. |
| `--iteration N` | Loop iteration number. Default: `0`. |

**Example:**

```bash
smithers revert workflow.tsx --run-id abc123 --node-id analyze --attempt 2
```

Exits with `0` on success, `1` on failure.

---

### cancel

Cancel a running or waiting-approval workflow. Marks all in-progress attempts as cancelled and sets the run status to `"cancelled"`.

```bash
smithers cancel <workflow.tsx> --run-id ID
```

**Example:**

```bash
smithers cancel workflow.tsx --run-id abc123
```

Output:

```json
{
  "runId": "abc123",
  "status": "cancelled",
  "cancelledAttempts": 2
}
```

Exits with code `2` (cancelled).

---

### version

Print the package version and exit.

```bash
smithers --version
smithers -v
```

---

### help

Print usage information and exit.

```bash
smithers --help
smithers -h
```

## Global Options

These options apply to `run` and `resume` commands:

| Option | Description | Default |
|---|---|---|
| `--root PATH` | Root directory for the tool sandbox. All filesystem tools are confined to this directory. | Workflow file's parent directory |
| `--log-dir PATH` | Directory for NDJSON event log files. Relative paths are resolved from `--root`. | `.smithers/executions/<runId>/logs` |
| `--no-log` | Disable writing event log files. Events are still persisted to the SQLite database. | Logging enabled |
| `--allow-network` | Allow the built-in `bash` tool to make outbound network requests. Does **not** affect CLI agent wrappers -- see [CLI Agents](/integrations/cli-agents) for agent-specific sandbox settings. | Disabled |
| `--max-output-bytes N` | Maximum bytes returned from a single tool call. | `200000` |
| `--tool-timeout-ms N` | Maximum time in milliseconds for a single tool call. | `60000` |
| `--max-concurrency N` | Maximum number of tasks running in parallel. | `4` |
| `--input JSON` | Input data as a JSON string. For `run`, provides the initial input; for `resume`, overrides the persisted input. | `{}` |
| `--run-id ID` | Explicit run identifier. | Auto-generated |
| `--resume` | Resume a previous run via the `run` command (requires `--run-id`). | Disabled |
| `--force` | Resume even if the run is still marked as running (for `resume` only). | Disabled |
| `--hot` | Enable [hot module replacement](/guides/hot-reload). Watches workflow files and reloads on save. In-flight tasks continue; new tasks use updated code. | Disabled |
| `--version`, `-v` | Print version and exit. | -- |
| `--help`, `-h` | Print help and exit. | -- |

## Exit Codes

| Code | Status | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `0` | `finished` | The workflow completed successfully. |
| `1` | `failed` | The workflow failed (task failure or engine error). |
| `2` | `cancelled` | The workflow was cancelled. |
| `3` | `waiting-approval` | The workflow is paused waiting for human approval. |
| `4` | *invalid input* | Invalid arguments, missing required flags, malformed JSON, or unknown command. |

## Workflow File
